author: @Buzzhumma
text: Maybe you can do it in reverse order by zinc plating stainless mesh as your tube and then separator and then ink coated dish cloth. The idea behind the stainless mesh is it can be re electro plated as the zinc is used up and re inserted like your cartridge idea. That should increase the energy density a lot.

author: @PressToDigitate
text:
As you may recall, I have a great interest in finding a proper form factor in which to implement an Aluminum/Urine/Hydroxide Fuel Cell, for the optimal extraction of energy from waste Aluminum cans ("Cannodes") and the...ahem...."user-supplied BioCatholyte".  There are good arguments for this, both in the applications (defense, emergency, camping), and in the chemistry/energetics itself (see Dr. Geraldine Botte's work on the efficiency of Hydrogen extraction from Urine).  I believe you have done experiments with scrap Aluminum in NaOH solution, and observed the prodigious evolution of both Heat and Hydrogen; in the proper apparatus, it should yield a significant Direct Current as well, at the same time.  


Dr. Botte has pursued Nickel-based catalysts for Urine (Urea) decomposition at the Cathode, and yet your nanostructured Carbon would be seemingly both much cheaper and easier to work with. (The Urine releases >20% more Hydrogen per liter than comparable salt water)  I have been interested in producing a batch of Iodized graphitic Carbon Nitride, for Cathode use (due to the citations of which you are well aware).  Might you have any data, anecdotes, or experience with the stability of your Graphene Inks in strong NaOH solutions? 


Given that the chemically-embodied electricity in a single standard waste 355ml Aluminum can is *200 Watt-hours* , and that scrap Aluminum is now down to $0.05/lb. in the U.S. (down from $0.50+/lb a few years ago), it makes far more sense to 'burn' empty cans in an electrochemical cell, for Power, Heat and Hydrogen, than to turn them in for conventional recycling. They can be crushed vertically into nicely uniform "biscuits".  But the best shape and configuration of the system still eludes me, and the versatility you've demonstrated with the inks might provide an answer, depending on the chemical stability in highly alkaline electrolyte.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have never tried it in high concentration alkaline environments - but I will now lol

replies.author: @PressToDigitate
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Note: The NaOH has proven to be a true catalyst for the Anode reaction, in Aluminum batteries/fuel cells, and is regenerated.   That was once in dispute, but there are papers on the subject out there establishing this.  I'd be curious to examine if Dr. Botte's Nickel catalyist for the Cathode  reaction could be dispersed in a Graphene matrix to good effect.

author: @William_Hada
text: Exciting videos Rob! Got some of your ink and can't wait to try this!  I have been wondering how does one determine if one has built an "air battery" or not? I guess air batteries are not rechargeable?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's an air battery when the cathode material is the air mate - the use of the ink as an apparent cathode is just that apparent - it is actually performing three main functions - current collector, site for the reaction and gas diffusion layer - the active material is oxygen from the air - hope this helps

author: @jamest.5001
text: Another idea, being you have the conductive ink.   With a few other inks, do you think you could "draw" a battery, or capacitor on paper? Layer the plates, and seperators, insulators, then when done add a drop of electrolyte to activate, it could power  low power devices, possibly emergency locator becons, radios, or it could be a new battery production? Or it could be simply insane.!!!.. Sometimes the difference between insanity and genious is a matter of opinion!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes mate - I can - I am about to do a video on just that - but the ink is freely available - you could investigate yourself too

author: @jamest.5001
text: What makes anything new difficult, is the fact that it's new, and unthought of! It's easy after its been done, to an extent anyway! It's less difficult, the knowing us the hard part in most cases! Trying to think through all the possible situations and all possible outcomes!! I think the better battery regardless of chemistry, is aeach cell built as if it is many low current high capacity cells. Built together for a higher current capabilities. This should allow for high current fast charging , and high current discharge. It may be nothing new,  just something I been thinking of while building battery packs, a battery built from many hi capacity, low current  cells, would possibly perform. Better than the same Batt capacity in a battery with less cell, it works the battery harder  the battery built with more cells will have it easier. Like I said , just a thought, so why wouldn't work at a smaller level, ?  I may be completely backwards here, or may not be explaining my meaning well enough to get my point across. Cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the real thing mate is the mentally trying to think things through takes ages and your mind wanders down many paths most of them a waste of time - very often - and something we seem to be forgetting is the mindset of the engineer - give it a go and see - most of the questions resolve themselves or are just not important
